name: Direct Feature Preference Elicitation
core_claim: Direct feature (attribute) preference elicitation asks the cold-start user about CONTENT FEATURES rather than titles or psychological traits: 'do you prefer shows with happy endings?', 'how much does gore bother you?', 'do you like slow, character-driven stories or fast-paced plots?', 'how dark do you want the tone?'. The theoretical link to enjoyment is the most direct of any content-light method in this research: the elicited variables ARE the descriptive dimensions over which content is indexed (genre, tone, pacing, violence/sexual-content tolerance, ending valence, complexity), so a stated feature preference maps onto a candidate item's tagged features with no intervening latent-construct inference. Unlike personality instruments (which predict feature preference probabilistically, e.g. Openness -> nonlinear-storytelling at r~0.2-0.3) and unlike item-based elicitation (which reveals feature preference observationally via the tags of chosen titles), direct feature elicitation skips both the trait detour and the title-recognition dependency by querying the feature axis directly. Its central claim is that users can introspect and accurately report which content features they enjoy or avoid, and that those self-reports locate them usefully in the same feature/tag space the recommender ranks over. Its differentiator versus item-based is scalability and catalogue-agnosticism (the question 'do you like happy endings?' is the same regardless of catalogue, language, or whether the user has heard of any title); its acknowledged liability is that stated feature preferences are systematically distorted by acquiescence bias, social-desirability bias, and the stated-versus-revealed gap, and that users often cannot introspect their own feature preferences accurately.
predictive_validity_for_media: The empirical literature treats attribute/feature elicitation as MORE informative per question than single-item ratings for cold-start collaborative filtering: eliciting preferences on item attributes (e.g. genre) in addition to or instead of item ratings improves early clustering and recommendation accuracy for cold-start users, because one attribute answer constrains a large region of item space at once whereas one item rating constrains a point (Pairwise and Attribute-Aware Decision Tree-Based Preference Elicitation for Cold-Start Recommendation, arXiv:2510.27342, 2025; Eliciting Auxiliary Information for Cold Start User Recommendation: A Survey, Applied Sciences 2021, MDPI 9608). Language-/attribute-based preference statements were shown competitive with item-based collaborative filtering in the near-cold-start regime while being far more explainable (Large Language Models are Competitive Near Cold-start Recommenders for Language- and Item-based Preferences, arXiv:2307.14225). On the GRANULARITY of prediction: direct feature elicitation predicts GENRE preference well (genre is literally the question asked), predicts THEMATIC and NARRATIVE-STYLE preference moderately when the questions target those axes (tone, pacing, ending valence, complexity), and is the only content-light method that can directly capture hard CONTENT FILTERS (gore tolerance, sexual content, on-screen violence) which act as veto constraints rather than ranking signals. COMPLETION-RATE prediction is not established and should be treated as unsupported. The dominant empirical caveat is the stated-revealed preference gap: across domains, what users SAY they prefer diverges systematically from what they actually choose and finish, because self-reports are filtered through self-image and social norms (Combining stated and revealed preferences, arXiv:2507.13552; engagement vs. stated-preference divergence, NIH PMC11894805). No clean effect-size estimate for 'stated feature preference -> joint TV/anime enjoyment' exists in the literature; the gain over a popularity prior is plausible but unquantified for this specific construct. [uncertain]
instrument_design: There is no single canonical validated instrument; direct feature elicitation is a design pattern, so item count and format are product choices rather than psychometric constraints. Typical forms: (a) Likert agreement on feature statements ('I enjoy shows with ambiguous endings' 1-5) — lowest engineering cost, highest acquiescence-bias exposure; (b) forced-choice / this-or-that ('Slow burn or fast-paced?', 'Light or dark?') — reduces scale-use and acquiescence bias, more engaging, the format most consumer onboarding quizzes use; (c) sliders on bipolar feature axes (e.g. cosy<->intense, realistic<->fantastical, light<->dark) — intuitive and compact; (d) tolerance/veto questions ('How much does graphic violence bother you?' never/sometimes/fine) — uniquely able to set hard content filters. MINIMUM VIABLE under-2-min / 6-10 item version for low-friction onboarding: 6-10 forced-choice or slider items on the highest-signal, most-introspectable axes — tone (light/dark), pacing (slow-burn/fast), ending valence (happy/bittersweet/downbeat), emotional intensity (cosy/intense), realism (grounded/fantastical), complexity (straightforward/demanding), plus 1-2 explicit content-tolerance vetoes (gore, sexual content). Forced-choice/sliders are strongly preferred over agree-disagree Likert here specifically because acquiescence bias is the headline threat (below). Completion time is well under two minutes; the format is intrinsically more engaging than a personality scale because every question is visibly about the content the user wants.
cold_start_applicability: This is one of the most directly applicable content-light cold-start methods, and arguably the best-suited of all to HelpME2C's specific constraints. At t=0 with zero viewing history it yields immediately usable signal because the elicited features ARE the recommender's indexing dimensions, so a feature-preference vector can be matched against tagged candidate items with no learned bridge. Its decisive advantage over item-based elicitation in the cold-start context is catalogue- and recognition-INDEPENDENCE: 'do you like happy endings?' requires no knowledge of any title, so it does not collapse for users new to a medium or region the way a recognisable-title seed grid does. This makes it the strongest content-light method for CROSS-MEDIUM inference: a stated preference for slow-burn, bittersweet, character-driven content transfers cleanly from TV to anime because both media are tagged on the same feature axes, whereas liking a specific TV title does not transfer without a learned mapping. The signal-quality ceiling is set not by applicability but by introspective accuracy and bias (users mis-state feature preferences, and some enjoyment-relevant features are not consciously accessible). Net: high applicability, catalogue-agnostic, strong cross-medium transfer, but the raw signal must be treated as noisy stated preference to be corrected by revealed behaviour as soon as interaction data arrives.
group_compatibility_use: For the HelpME2C two-person core this method extends more cleanly than either personality vectors or item picks, because the elicited unit (feature preferences) is both directly combinable AND directly interpretable as a joint constraint. Two feature-preference vectors over the same axes can be aggregated by the standard group-recommendation strategies (rating-, result-, or model-aggregation taxonomy; Group Recommender Systems: Aggregation, Satisfaction and Group Attributes, Springer 2015): (1) AVERAGE the two vectors per feature axis — maximises mean fit, smooths disagreement; (2) INTERSECTION / shared-preference — features both partners endorse become strong positives, sharpest joint signal but sparse for dissimilar pairs; (3) LEAST-MISERY — for each candidate, score by the less-satisfied partner's feature fit, which the dyad literature favours because one unhappy member in a two-person group is highly salient and cannot be outvoted (least-misery = minimum member satisfaction; Towards Data Science group-recsys overview; Springer aggregation chapter). Feature elicitation has a UNIQUE two-person advantage absent from other methods: hard content VETOES compose with set semantics — if either partner says graphic gore is a dealbreaker, gore-tagged content is filtered for the dyad regardless of the other's tolerance. This is exactly the asymmetric-constraint case least-misery/veto logic handles well and averaging handles badly (averaging a 'fine with gore' and a 'never' yields a nonsense midpoint). A dyad-friendly UX is joint elicitation where both partners answer the same short feature quiz and the system surfaces agreements, tensions, and shared vetoes. Direct evidence that a specific aggregation rule maximises MEASURED joint viewing satisfaction for two-person dyads is thin; treat the rule (likely least-misery for ranking + hard-veto intersection for filters) as an A/B-testable hyperparameter. [uncertain]
quiz_ux_implications: This is a near-ideal consumer onboarding quiz format on engagement grounds — every question is transparently about the content the user wants, so reactance is low and the task is intrinsically motivating, like a 'find your taste' quiz — but it is the method MOST exposed to response biases, which is the central UX design problem. (1) ACQUIESCENCE BIAS is the headline threat: agree-disagree feature statements ('I enjoy complex narratives') invite disproportionate agreement regardless of true preference, inflating endorsement of every feature (Acquiescence bias, Wikipedia; Lensym guide). MITIGATION: avoid agree-disagree Likert entirely; use FORCED-CHOICE this-or-that and bipolar sliders, which have no 'agree' option to default to. (2) SOCIAL-DESIRABILITY / SELF-IMAGE BIAS: users over-claim sophisticated taste (complex, challenging, slow-burn, foreign) and under-claim guilty-pleasure taste (trashy, formulaic, comfort-watch), reproducing the stated-vs-revealed gap (Combining stated and revealed preferences, arXiv:2507.13552; The Tale of Two Preferences, Medium). MITIGATION: frame questions around mood/occasion ('tonight do you want easy comfort or something demanding?') rather than identity ('are you a sophisticated viewer?'), which reduces the self-presentation incentive. (3) INTROSPECTION LIMIT: users genuinely cannot report feature preferences they have never reflected on, and may not know key attributes exist (users searching for recommendations may not have deep domain knowledge or be aware of relevant attributes; ACM TORS 3629981). MITIGATION: keep to the most concretely-experienced axes (tone, pacing, gore tolerance, ending valence) and avoid abstract craft attributes. Friction-minimising format: 6-10 forced-choice/slider items, mood-framed, with the stated vector treated as a prior to be overwritten by revealed behaviour, never as ground truth.
cross_cultural_validity: Direct feature elicitation has a strong structural cross-cultural ADVANTAGE over both item-based elicitation and personality scales, with one important residual risk. Advantage: unlike a recognisable-title seed grid (whose globally-popular pool is Western/English-dominant and mislocates non-Western users), a feature question like 'do you prefer happy or bittersweet endings?' is catalogue-agnostic and equally answerable by a Korean, Japanese, Hindi, or Arabic-first user — so it does not systematically underserve the multilingual catalogue the way item-based does. And unlike personality instruments, it does not depend on measurement invariance of a latent Western-derived construct. Residual risks: (a) feature SEMANTICS and DESIRABILITY norms shift across cultures — 'dark', 'slow', 'happy ending', and especially gore/sexual-content tolerance carry different connotations and social-desirability pressures by culture, so the social-desirability distortion above is itself culturally variable; (b) some feature axes are medium- and culture-specific (the cosy/healing 'iyashikei' axis in anime, melodrama conventions in K-drama, telenovela tropes) and a Western-default question set can miss axes that matter to non-Western viewers; (c) acquiescence bias itself varies cross-culturally (higher in some collectivist and high-context cultures), compounding the bias problem unevenly. Net: the catalogue-agnosticism makes this the most cross-culturally portable content-light elicitation method, but the question set must be localised in WORDING and partly in AXES, and bias correction must be applied per-locale rather than globally. [uncertain]
privacy_consent_implications: Lower-risk than personality profiling but not risk-free. The data collected is declared content-feature preference (tone, pacing, gore tolerance, ending valence), which is ordinary personal data on its face, not GDPR Article 9 special-category data. The caveat under the ICO 'intentional inference' test: certain feature/tolerance answers can act as proxies for special-category attributes (e.g. strong tolerance/aversion patterns around sexual content, violence, or religious/political themes could imply protected characteristics), so if HelpME2C ever intentionally derives such inferences the data tips toward Article 9 — but for entertainment ranking the intended use stays ordinary. Because the questions are self-evidently purposeful (the user is visibly tuning their own recommendations), clear freely-given consent at the point of collection is well-supported and reactance is low; still requires a purpose statement and the derived vector must be deletable/hard-deleted per ADR-0012 DSAR rules. Re-identification risk is modest (a short feature-preference vector is far less uniquely identifying than a clinical trait profile). It still touches privacy-control flows (CLAUDE.md §4), so confirm before building, and store the derived prior rather than retaining raw sensitive-tolerance answers longer than needed.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: This method maps to the HelpME2C theme vocabulary the MOST DIRECTLY of any in this research, because several elicited feature axes ARE theme-vocabulary axes or map onto specific slugs with little inference. Tone/darkness axis: 'dark' preference -> dark-comedy, existential-horror, cosmic-horror, folk-horror, moral-compromise, complicity-with-evil, dystopian-society, war-and-its-toll; 'light' preference -> absurdist-comedy, found-family, coming-of-age, fish-out-of-water. Pacing axis: slow-burn preference -> slow-burn, character-study, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death, biographical-portrait; fast-paced -> chase-and-escape, heist-and-caper, survival-and-endurance, single-incident-fallout. Ending-valence axis: bittersweet/downbeat tolerance -> grief-and-loss, unrequited-longing, rise-and-fall, vengeance-and-its-cost, loneliness-and-isolation, earned-ambiguity (as a texture/ending property); happy-ending preference -> redemption-arc, underdog-rise, platonic-soulmates. Complexity axis: high-complexity preference -> nonlinear-storytelling, ensemble-mosaic, magical-realism, ethical-dilemma, propaganda-and-truth; low -> workplace-procedural, whodunit, mystery-investigation. Content-tolerance vetoes (gore/violence/sexual content) act as FILTERS over slugs rather than ranking weights: low gore tolerance suppresses existential-horror, cosmic-horror, war-and-its-toll, survival-and-endurance, vengeance-and-its-cost. The key structural point: direct feature elicitation populates the theme-preference vector by NEAR-DIRECT MAPPING (the answer names the axis), whereas item-based populates it observationally (read tags off picked titles) and personality populates it probabilistically (trait -> slug correlation). The theme vocabulary is therefore the natural representation for storing the output of a feature quiz, and the shared feature/theme axes are exactly what carry a TV preference across the medium boundary to anime.
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE as a core onboarding component, paired with item-based elicitation as the two pillars of HelpME2C's cold-start, with personality instruments treated as optional/experimental supplements rather than primary. Rationale: feature elicitation fills item-based's two structural blind spots exactly — it is catalogue/recognition-independent (works for users who recognise no seed titles) and it transfers cleanly across the TV/anime medium boundary via shared feature axes — while item-based fills feature elicitation's blind spot (revealed-preference grounding that corrects stated-preference bias). Recommended design: 6-10 FORCED-CHOICE / slider items (NOT agree-disagree Likert, to defeat acquiescence bias) on the most introspectable, highest-signal axes: tone (light/dark), pacing (slow-burn/fast), ending valence, emotional intensity (cosy/intense), realism (grounded/fantastical), complexity, plus 1-2 explicit content-TOLERANCE vetoes (gore, sexual content). Frame questions by mood/occasion rather than identity to suppress social-desirability bias. Place it at onboarding alongside or just before a region/language-aware item-pick grid; write its output straight into the theme-preference vector via the near-direct slug mappings above. CRITICAL: treat the stated feature vector as a noisy PRIOR to be down-weighted and overwritten by revealed behaviour as soon as interaction data accrues; do not treat it as ground truth. For the two-person core: run JOINT feature elicitation, aggregate ranking via least-misery, and compose content vetoes with set-union (either partner's hard veto filters the dyad). Localise question wording and partly the axis set per locale (e.g. add a cosy/healing axis for anime-leaning users). No hard dependency on other instruments — it stands alone and is in fact the recommended catalogue-agnostic complement to item picks. North-Star trace: 'a 60-second joint feature quiz gives both partners a catalogue-agnostic, theme-grounded shared preference seed — including hard content vetoes — so their first joint recommendation is plausibly watchable across both TV and anime before any viewing history exists' — directly traceable.
key_sources: Free / open access: 'Pairwise and Attribute-Aware Decision Tree-Based Preference Elicitation for Cold-Start Recommendation', arXiv:2510.27342 (2025) — attribute vs item-rating elicitation, https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.27342 . 'Eliciting Auxiliary Information for Cold Start User Recommendation: A Survey', Applied Sciences 2021, MDPI, open access — https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/11/20/9608 . 'Seamlessly Unifying Attributes and Items: Conversational Recommendation for Cold-Start Users', arXiv:2005.12979 — attribute-then-item elicitation, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2005.12979 . 'Generating Usage-related Questions for Preference Elicitation in Conversational Recommender Systems', ACM TORS 2024 — direct attribute questioning and its cognitive-bias pitfalls, https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3629981 . 'Large Language Models are Competitive Near Cold-start Recommenders for Language- and Item-based Preferences', arXiv:2307.14225 — language/attribute vs item preference, explainability. 'A First Look at Selection Bias in Preference Elicitation for Recommendation', RecSys CONSEQUENCES 2023, arXiv:2405.00554 — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2405.00554 . 'Combining stated and revealed preferences', arXiv:2507.13552 — stated-vs-revealed gap, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.13552 . 'Engagement, user satisfaction, and the amplification of divisive content on social media', NIH PMC11894805 — engagement diverges from stated preference, https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11894805/ . Acquiescence bias background (free): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acquiescence_bias ; https://lensym.com/blog/acquiescence-bias-guide . Group aggregation (mixed access): 'Group Recommender Systems: Aggregation, Satisfaction and Group Attributes', Springer 2015 (paywalled), https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-7637-6_22 ; 'An overview of consensus models for group decision-making and group recommender systems', UMUAI 2023, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11257-023-09380-z ; 'An introduction to group recommender systems', Towards Data Science (free) — average / least-misery / most-pleasure strategies. 'The effect of preference elicitation methods on the user experience of a recommender system', CHI 2010 EA — https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1753846.1754001 .
